Name: gpu_inference_optimization
Title: Kernel-Level Throughput Engineering for Efficient Deep Learning Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing fused CUDA/Triton kernels, memory-bandwidth utilization, KV-cache layout, and tensor-core occupancy, we can achieve significant end-to-end speedups for deep learning inference on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Previous work has focused on optimizing deep learning models for inference, but few have explored the potential of kernel-level throughput engineering on commodity GPUs. Our approach differs from existing work in its focus on empirical, hardware-faithful evaluation and optimization.
Abstract: This proposal presents a novel approach to optimizing deep learning inference on commodity GPUs. By applying kernel-level throughput engineering techniques, such as optimizing memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy, we aim to achieve significant end-to-end speedups. Our approach prioritizes empirical, hardware-faithful evaluation, using tools like nvprof and Nsight to measure and optimize kernel performance. We will conduct experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of our approach, reporting metrics such as p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Experiments: Optimize memory-bandwidth utilization by reordering kernel operations and reducing HBM round-trips
Improve tensor-core occupancy by tuning thread block sizes and register allocation
Evaluate the impact of quantization, sparsity, and layer-skipping on latency and accuracy
Compare the performance of our optimized kernels against state-of-the-art baselines
Risk Factors and Limitations: Limited access to specialized hardware or expertise may hinder the optimization process
The complexity of modern deep learning models may make it challenging to identify optimal kernel configurations
The proposed approach may not generalize to all types of deep learning models or hardware platforms
